sentences.text: At the beginning, it was fair.
Because I was very lucky, and I was able to work in the kitchen.
So as little food as we had there and was bad it was, I still had just a little bit more.
But once you were in the camp about four, five, six weeks-- the camp was infested with lice.
And typhus fever was going through the whole camp-- which people died day by day, all over.
And of course, they did not bury the people.
So that brought in more infections and diseases.
And I also had typhoid fever.
I was very sick.
But I was very lucky that somebody who I worked with in the kitchen got to like me very much.
And she would not let me stay in my barrack.
Because if I stayed in my barrack, I would be thrown out in the mountains of dead and leave there to die.
She would put me on her back, carry me to the kitchen, let me sit there.
And when the SS came-- and they saw the SS coming from the outside-- they stood me up quick and said, now you stand right here.
Here is a knife or a spoon.
And do the cooking.
And this is how I survived.
And then at night, she would take me back on her back.
So after a few days, the crisis was over.
And I survived.
No, really not.
It was a Polish girl, somebody whom I never knew before.
Not at that point yet.
They said that somehow, the Germans are not winning the war.
And something will happened.
And then one day-- of course, things got so very bad that we had absolutely nothing to eat anymore.
But not only we didn't have anything to eat, even the SS didn't have anything to eat.
There was no food.
And one day, the news came that we're not supposed to touch the water.
Because girls who drank the water died instantly.
So we knew the water was poisoned.
The Germans poisoned the water.
And about two days later, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was in the kitchen early in the morning.
We had to be there at 5 o'clock in the morning, even though we had nothing to do there, nothing to cook.
There was absolutely nothing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The news came that there is a white flag far away.
And we can see it.
And the Germans are giving up.
So naturally, I had to see it.
I went out.
And we saw the German soldiers and the German women soldiers running, running.
And I came out.
I wanted to see the white flag.
And I saw it, but only for a minute because came a German soldier and beat me up so terribly I wasn't able to get up.
And the girls saw this, watching from the kitchen.
So one ran out and pulled me back.
But I saw the white flag far away.
And then about two, three hours later, we heard on the loudspeaker that you are free.
We are English soldiers.
We came to free you.
And the water is poisoned.
But we're bringing in water soon.
And nobody should touch anything.
And we're going to help you.
And the first thing we saw-- they brought in these huge water tanks, you know, cars with huge tanks.
And we all stood by there and was drinking the water.
And, of course, they opened all the gates.
And suddenly-- and, and then they, they brought us food, all kinds of food.
We had bread, which we didn't eat for a long time.
And all of a sudden, we were running around like wild-- the gates were open.
The men came in from the other lagers.
And we were going looking who was alive, who would we meet?
And the boys opened all the magazine doors, mostly the Polish boys.
And we went in, and we got clothes, clean clothes.
And the first thing the English did-- well, after, after we lined up for the water, we had to line up.
And they sprayed us with a white powder, which we didn't know at that time, what it was.
But all of a sudden, we had no lice.
We were free from all that.
Yes, it was DDT, which we thought it was the most miracle kind of a thing they brought us.
And of course, we were very, very happy.
We got water first.
And we got rid of our lice.
And then we were looking for people.
And we were fed.
One tragedy was-- which was actually the biggest tragedy-- that the English were-- wanted to be good to us and brought us all kinds of very heavy food, the canned meat.
And these people were starving for weeks and weeks, didn't eat-- and weren't able to eat it.
The minute they ate it, they had diarrhea.
And they passed away-- a couple of days later, passed away.
My luck was that I always said one thing-- I only want one extra slice of bread, that I wouldn't be able to eat anymore I'd be so full.
And the only thing I wanted-- bread and potatoes.
I don't know why.
That was the only thing I ate.
I used to get up in the middle of the night.
And I make myself sandwiches.
We had bread, these long bread.
I cut it lengthwise.
And there was one row of bread, one row of potatoes, one row of onions and again, bread, potatoes, onions.
And this went up high, as high as I could open my mouth.
And I used to eat it all night.
And I think that's what saved me.
I never wanted the meat or, or any food they cooked.
It just didn't impress me.
I just wanted bread and potatoes.
Nobody has an idea how delicious is a cooked, cold potato.
I love it.
And I love cooked, boiled potatoes-- cold.
It has a special taste.
About 10 days later, we were registered, of course, by the English.
And then they were-- we were put over to another camp for a-- the camp's name was Celle, C-- C-E-L-L-E, which was near Bergen-Belsen.
And it was a Nazi camp.
They put us over there for quarantine to make sure that the sick people will get into hospitals or taken away.
A lot of people then-- that's where they took people to Sweden and Switzerland and all over, you know.
They took care of the sick people.
And they helped the ones-- fairly healthy ones, those who were able to walk and be on their own feet.
After two weeks, they let them go.
They let them go home.
Look, I was one of the lucky ones who was fairly healthy.
And since I was born in Czechoslovakia-- that was the first-- the-- first the Czechs they let go home.
Because the Czechs weren't enemy.
Hungarians were enemy to the English.
But the Czechs weren't.
So after two weeks, I was able to leave.
I didn't know where I was going to go.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm sorry, I want to go back for a few minutes to Celle.
I found two of my nieces in Bergen-Belsen alive.
At the end of Bergen-Belsen, they were brought in-- I don't know from Mauthausen, or which camp.
And I found the two girls there.
I tried to give them some food.
And then we went-- we were liberated.
They were sick.
And they came with me to Celle.
And unfortunately, from the food, they both died.
Yeah, Eva and Klara Ehrman.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I had to put them out to the mountain of dead-- among the mountain of dead.
Because there was no barrier.
I didn't have enough strength.
I asked somebody else.
And we just put them on top of the others-- two beautiful, young girls.
And then I packed up and I went to look-- I went-- I wanted to go home to see if maybe a miracle happened.
Maybe my brother came home.
Maybe my father-- maybe I'll find my home.
Since I didn't have enough-- any money, and I didn't have anything, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I traveled on top of the train.
Because I had no money to buy a ticket.
And the conductor did not come on top of the train.
My first stop was Prague.
In Prague, the Joint was waiting for us.
They waited for us with food.
And they gave us $10 apiece.
Because there were other girls coming home with me.
I wasn't going alone.
So from the $10, we were able to go further.
We had good food.
We had a couple of meals.
And then we got on top of the train and went further.
Was that dangerous, being on top of the train?
Yes, it was.
But after what we went through, nothing was dangerous.
It was nothing.
As long as we didn't have to pay, we, we were happy.
We had no money.
And then [SIGHS] I went home to my town, to Ujhely-- Satoraljaujhely, where I lived and where I was hoping-- unfortunately, I found nobody and nothing.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the home was destroyed.
The synagogue was destroyed.
It was a, a dead ghost town.
It was terrible.
I was liberated the end of April, around 1928.
And then for two weeks, we were in quarantine.
It was the, the very last days of April, maybe the first couple of days in May, first few days in May.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When I went to my hometown and I found nothing, the only thing-- oh, I went with one of the girls from my town with whom I was together.
And as we arrived, she got word that some of her brothers and a cousin was home.
And we went to her house.
And sure enough, we found four boys, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one of them or two of them who came back from the concentration camp, and one or two of them who came back from Siberia.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they took in everybody.
Their name was Eisenberg-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] very wonderful four boys.
And they gave us food.
They let me sleep there.
They had so many people that everybody was sleeping on the floor-- man, woman, whoever.
It didn't matter.
And everybody had what to eat.
They had a big table.
And in the morning-- full of bread and coffee, and we had what to eat.
They were wonderful, wonderful people.
And a matter of fact, one of them lives in Miami and is here.
And I'm friends with him, [?
Manu ?] Eisenberg.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] After a few days, I had to go further.
I know I couldn't stay there in my town where there was nobody and nothing.
My home was destroyed.
I found absolutely nothing.
So again, on top of the train-- that was my bedroom, my living room, and everything [LAUGHS]-- climbed up on top of the train, and I headed for Budapest.
I knew that there were some people-- by that time, I heard-- you know, people were talking.
And I met people-- that they did not take out all the Jews from Budapest.
They had their ghettos.
And my mother had a wonderful friend who was a far relative.
And my mother used to take me up to Budapest.
And I knew where she lived and who they were.
So I went to her house.
I found her.
And when they saw me at the door, they started to cry because they had one child, one son, who was killed by the Nazis, shot into the Danau-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] their one and only child.
And when they saw me, they said, my God, God sent you back to us to make up for my son.
And they took me in.
And they took wonderful care of me.
Oh sure, Ella and Jula Friedrich.
Almost until I got married.
That became my home.
They became everything to me.
They were very, very good to me.
They did not have much because that time, Budapest had very little.
Everything was rationed.
But the little they had, they shared with me.
And the first bath I took there.
And the first piece of clean underwear-- and I slept in a bed.
And I was treated the first time as a human being.
They were very, very good to me.
Yes, a little after that.
I was working-- I tried to get them some food.
To reciprocate, I used to go back on top of the train again to my hometown and get some food from the villages, bring up certain things.
And one day, this fellow showed up, was looking for relatives, and found me.
This fellow's [LAUGHS] name is Leo Rapaport.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I knew him about three months.
And after that, I married him.
I was--
--married the 2nd of May, 1946.
He was also home looking for family.
After the marriage, we wanted to come to America.
We both decided we wanted to come to America.
But I got married in Romania because that's where he lived.
And he used to come up to Budapest to do some business.
We knew that from Romania or Hungary, it would take years and years and years to come to America.
But he found out that if we went back to the concentration camp where we were liberated, there, we were privileged.
And we would be able to come to America because the Americans gave privilege to the people who were liberated.
And the quota was going first to them.
So we smuggled ourself back to the camp.
And--
It was terrible.
We went back to my hometown because I had connection there.
And there was the border to Czechoslovakia.
So I was able to give some money.
And at night, they let us go through to Czechoslovakia.
And from Czechoslovakia, we also knew somebody who, for money, smuggled us back to camp in a terrible, rainy, cool night.
And they were shooting after us.
And we went down in the mud and crawled across the border.
The Czechs.
The borders were closed.
You couldn't come and go.
You needed visas, passports, money.
We had nothing.
So we crawled back.
And we went back to a camp called Pocking, where they had all these people concentrated who were liberated, and they didn't have where to go.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then my husband started to look around where we should register, how we could get out to America.
And somebody gave us the idea that we should go to Stuttgart because they're already-- most of the people in Stuttgart are Polish.
And the Czech quota is very good.
And that's where we went.
And then, really, in a few months, we got our visa.
And we were able to come to America.
We came to Brooklyn.
My husband's previous partner, a very close friend, was waiting for us.
And we came to them.
We lived with them for a few months until we were able to get on our own feet.
And my husband started working.
And until-- I became pregnant.
And I was able to borrow some money and get ourselves a little home and start a life.
My husband was from Romania.
His name was Leo Rapaport, R-A-P-A-P-O-R-T.
And he-- his hometown was Satu Mare, which sometimes was Romanian, sometimes was Hungary.
At that moment after World War II, it became Romania again.
Oh yes.
He was a survivor.
He was also at the same time for one year in camp.
And--
He was in Dachau.
And he was in various other camps.
And he was liberated in Feldafing by the Americans.
Yes.
Yes.
Thank God he was.
He was a very hard worker.
He was very young when he started his business in Europe.
And he found his connections.
After he went back to Europe, everybody was very happy to see him, that he survived.
And they helped him very much to establish himself in America.
Oh, well, later on-- I have to mention it, that when we came to America, we came on a Navy boat with the help of Joint.
And I think my husband is perhaps [LAUGHS] the one and only one-- that when we had a little money, he went up to the Joint.
And he said, I want to pay for my wife's and my fare.
And he did.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yes, later on, thank God, when he was doing fairly well, the first thing we did, we tried to help Israel.
We tried to help all the charity organizations.
And mostly, we helped to build schools.
Because we both felt that, in life, the most important thing is education.
If you have education, you will be able to become somebody and continue.
And perhaps because I did not have a chance to complete my education because it was interrupted, I felt so strong that we should build schools.
We actually built three schools, two in Israel and one in New York-- in Monsey, New York, where we lived.
We helped to build the school.
And I dedicated myself to work for that school for many, many years and establish it.
We started with 120 or 130 students.
And today, they have over a thousand.
And they have a few extra buildings.
